title: Polydactyly & Syndactyly: A Systematic Approach
objectives: Understand the pathophysiology of Polydactyly & Syndactyly
Identify key diagnostic features of Polydactyly & Syndactyly
Apply clinical reasoning to Polydactyly & Syndactyly management
Recognize common pitfalls in Polydactyly & Syndactyly

sections.title: Overview of Polydactyly & Syndactyly
sections.content:
**Polydactyly & Syndactyly**

Digital ray formation, apoptosis in web spaces, preaxial vs postaxial polydactyly associations

**Key Points:**
• Digital rays form from limb bud mesenchyme; programmed cell death (apoptosis) separates digits
• Syndactyly: failure of apoptosis between digital rays (cutaneous vs osseous)
• Polydactyly: extra digits; Postaxial (ulnar/fibular side) most common, often isolated/autosomal dominant in Black populations

sections.title: Pathophysiology & Key Features
sections.content:
• Digital rays form from limb bud mesenchyme; programmed cell death (apoptosis) separates digits
• Syndactyly: failure of apoptosis between digital rays (cutaneous vs osseous)

sections.title: Diagnosis & Management
sections.content:
• Polydactyly: extra digits; Postaxial (ulnar/fibular side) most common, often isolated/autosomal dominant in Black populations
• Preaxial polydactyly (radial/tibial): more likely associated with syndromes (Holt-Oram, Fanconi)
• Syndactyly of 2nd-3rd toes is extremely common and usually requires no treatment

sections.title: Clinical Pearls
sections.content:
**High-Yield Points for Polydactyly & Syndactyly:**

• Polydactyly: extra digits; Postaxial (ulnar/fibular side) most common, often isolated/autosomal dominant in Black populations
• Preaxial polydactyly (radial/tibial): more likely associated with syndromes (Holt-Oram, Fanconi)
• Syndactyly of 2nd-3rd toes is extremely common and usually requires no treatment

options.explanation: Syndactyly of 2nd-3rd toes is extremely common and usually requires no treatment
